etymology.origin_note: Originally an adjective meaning 'without reason, irrational' (ἀ- privative + λόγος 'reason, speech'). The semantic shift to 'horse' occurred in Byzantine Greek when τὸ ἄλογον 'the irrational [creature]' became the standard word for horse, displacing classical ἵππος. Horses were the archetypal irrational animals, lacking human λόγος.

diachrony.meaning: irrational, without reason, contrary to logic
diachrony.note: Philosophical term used by Plato and Aristotle for the non-rational parts of soul or nature
Neuter substantive narrowed from any irrational animal to horse, replacing ἵππος as the common word
Complete lexicalization; etymological sense unknown to most speakers

cognates.note: Same ἀ- + logic construction, preserves the original 'without reason' sense lost in Greek
Contains ἵππος, the classical word that άλογο replaced
From PIE *h₁éḱwos; Romance languages kept this root while Greek took the 'irrational animal' path
Contains same ἀ- prefix but from λόγος 'ratio', meaning proportional

quotes.translation_en: the irrational part of the soul

register.usage_note: Standard modern word for horse; ἵππος survives only in learned compounds like ιπποδρόμιο (racecourse) and ιπποπόταμος (hippopotamus)

etymology.origin_note: From PIE *leǵ- 'to collect, gather,' extended to 'speak' (speaking as gathering words). Homer uses it for speech, tale, and reckoning. Heraclitus elevates it to cosmic organizing principle. By the classical period it encompasses reason, argument, ratio, proportion, and any form of structured rational discourse.

diachrony.note: Everyday language: what is said, a story told, or a numerical calculation

diachrony.meaning: rational discourse, argument, reason, proportion, account
diachrony.note: Heraclitus' cosmic principle; Plato's dialectic; Aristotle's logic; mathematical ratio
John 1:1 fuses Greek philosophical logos with Hebrew dabar (divine word/deed), making it theological
Patristic literature uses it for homilies and theological arguments

diachrony.meaning: word, reason, speech, account; 'λόγω' (by reason of)
diachrony.note: Full semantic range preserved from everyday speech to philosophical weight

cognates.note: From λογική (τέχνη), the art of reasoning
κατά + λόγος, a complete listing or account
Same PIE root with sense 'gather' → 'gather written words' → 'read'

quotes.translation_en: In the beginning was the Word

quotes.text: τοῦ λόγου δὲ ἐόντος ξυνοῦ ζώουσιν οἱ πολλοὶ ὡς ἰδίαν ἔχοντες φρόνησιν
quotes.translation_en: Though the Logos is common, most live as if they had a private understanding

quotes.text: ὁ ἄνθρωπος φύσει πολιτικὸν ζῷον καὶ λόγον ἔχον
quotes.translation_en: Man is by nature a political animal and one possessing logos

register.usage_note: Retains full classical semantic range in modern Greek; equally comfortable in everyday speech (my word, his speech) and philosophical contexts (human reason, rational account)

etymology.origin_note: Direct descendant of PIE *ḱerd- 'heart,' preserved with remarkable stability across Indo-European languages. Used literally for the physical organ and figuratively for the emotional and intellectual center. In Homer, the heart thinks as well as feels. Modern form drops the iota subscript of ancient -ίᾳ.

diachrony.meaning: heart (physical organ), seat of thought, emotion, and will
diachrony.note: Homeric psychology locates both thinking and feeling in the heart

diachrony.meaning: heart (anatomical), seat of emotion, courage, desire
diachrony.note: Medical writers focus on physical organ; literary texts emphasize emotional center
New Testament uses it for moral/spiritual core: pure heart, hard heart, believing with the heart
Both medical anatomy and spiritual literature (hesychasm focuses on prayer of the heart)

diachrony.meaning: heart (anatomical and emotional), courage, core, center
diachrony.note: Full metaphorical range: love, courage, sincerity, center of anything

cognates.note: Germanic reflex of PIE *ḱerd- with identical semantic range
Via French cœur from Latin cor, meaning center/heart of something

quotes.translation_en: and his heart groaned within him
Blessed are the pure in heart

quotes.text: Στην καρδιά μου να έχω πάντα την Ιθάκη
quotes.translation_en: To always have Ithaca in my heart

register.usage_note: Equally natural for anatomical organ (heart attack, heart surgery) and emotional/metaphorical center (speak from the heart, lion-hearted, heart of the city); idiomatically productive

etymology.origin_note: From φίλος 'friend, dear one' (related to φιλέω 'to love') with abstract noun suffix -ία. Classical philosophy distinguishes φιλία (friendship-love between equals) from ἔρως (passionate desire) and ἀγάπη (selfless love). Aristotle devotes Nicomachean Ethics books 8-9 to analyzing three types of φιλία: based on utility, pleasure, or shared virtue.

diachrony.note: Central ethical concept; Aristotle distinguishes utility-friendship, pleasure-friendship, and virtue-friendship
NT rarely uses it (James 4:4 condemns 'friendship with the world'), preferring ἀγάπη for Christian love
Less philosophically technical; standard word for friendship alongside ἀγάπη
Standard word for friendship; lost classical philosophical granularity (Aristotle's three types no longer encoded in the word)

cognates.note: Productive in English for 'lover of X': bibliophile, Francophile, audiophile
Not cognate but semantic parallel; from amicus (friend), as φιλία from φίλος

quotes.text: ἡ φιλία τοῦ κόσμου ἔχθρα τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν
quotes.translation_en: Friendship with the world is enmity with God

register.usage_note: Slightly more formal or literary than simply φίλοι (friends); used to emphasize the quality, depth, or abstract concept of friendship rather than just naming the relationship
